episode.title: Cariboo Signals - Indigenous Lands & Innovation
episode.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Indigenous Lands & Innovation.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down [Williams Lake Tribune] B.C. First Nation at odds over ownership of historic Greater Victoria site and [Williams Lake Tribune] Barkerville 2026 season brings gold rush history to life with new attractions and events, plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: When Indigenous communities share ecological knowledge with Western institutions, how can that sharing preserve Indigenous data sovereignty and prevent knowledge systems from being absorbed or devalued? Topics include: Data sovereignty and decolonizing data systems, Knowledge braiding versus integration, Indigenous fire stewardship and clam garden restoration, Traditional ecological knowledge versus Western scientific monitoring, Food sovereignty and ecosystem restoration projects, Funding gaps in Guardian Stewards and community-controlled programs.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

COMMUNITY SPOTLIGHT: Denisiqi Services Society

From wildfires to clam gardens: decolonizing data
articles.summary: From wildfires to clam gardens: decolonizing data

articles.title: Braiding knowledge: how Indigenous expertise and western science are converging
articles.summary: Researchers are weaving Native practices with western methods to revive ecosystems and reclaim food sovereignty

articles.title: From wildfires to clam gardens: decolonizing data
articles.summary: From wildfires to clam gardens: decolonizing data

discussion.central_question: When Indigenous communities share ecological knowledge with Western institutions, how can that sharing preserve Indigenous data sovereignty and prevent knowledge systems from being absorbed or devalued?
discussion.riley_position: Indigenous and Western knowledge systems are complementary rather than substitutive; braiding them as distinct strands preserves the integrity of both, and communities should begin building governance and knowledge infrastructure now despite funding constraints, because the window for encoding intergenerational ecological understanding is closing.
riley_key_evidence: Selenium contamination cases where Indigenous community reports of sick animals and declining fish preceded formal monitoring data by years
Clam garden research between marine biologists and Lekwungen and W̱SÁNEĆ peoples shaped research questions from the nations first, not by university priorities
Guardian Stewards programs run by B.C. First Nations where data goes to the nation first before provincial/federal sharing
discussion.casey_position: Data sovereignty requires more than access control—it requires defining what counts as data first; communities must preserve decision-making control over the pace and architecture of knowledge work and avoid accepting under-resourced programs that create dependencies without leveraging power to negotiate terms.
casey_key_evidence: Broadband rollouts, environmental monitoring, and health data systems where short-term grants expire leaving communities with obligations but no infrastructure
Traditional ecological knowledge evolved in a climate regime that no longer fully applies; both Indigenous and Western systems are being stress-tested by novel conditions simultaneously
Guardian programs covering millions of hectares on budgets inadequate relative to scope, representing downloading of responsibility without resources
discussion.resolution: They converged on the principle that both knowledge systems are imperfect and complementary, neither sufficient alone; they agreed the epistemological framework—what counts as knowledge—must precede data architecture, and that this is a harder problem than governance because it requires different ways of knowing to coexist without one consuming the other.
topics_covered: Data sovereignty and decolonizing data systems
Indigenous fire stewardship and clam garden restoration
Traditional ecological knowledge versus Western scientific monitoring
Food sovereignty and ecosystem restoration projects
Funding gaps in Guardian Stewards and community-controlled programs
calls_to_action: A Cariboo community could bring together Elders, land stewards, and locally available technical capacity to spend real time answering the foundational question: what counts as data from an Indigenous stewardship perspective for this landscape, and what are the indicators that actually matter?—without immediately building a database, just establishing epistemology first so the architecture follows correctly.

Communities should preserve optionality by assessing what knowledge-sharing with provincial institutions actually costs before committing to short-term grant cycles, rather than accepting underfunded programs that lock in relationships before the community has leverage to negotiate terms.
Send feedback and perspectives on Indigenous data governance to feedback at cariboo signals dot c-a.
